disease.name: Anal Fissure
disease.note: Longitudinal tear in the anal canal distal to dentate line, most commonly at posterior midline (90%). The classic triad: severe pain on defecation, bright red bleeding on toilet paper, and sphincter spasm. In Kenyan patients, chronic constipation from low-fiber street food diets is a common precipitant. Lateral or anterior fissures raise suspicion for Crohn's disease.
pathophysiologyDetail: Ischemic watershed zone at posterior midline — the inferior rectal artery provides a poor blood supply to the posterior commissure, creating an anatomical locus minoris resistentiae that explains 90% posterior midline predilection

Internal anal sphincter hypertonicity — resting anal pressure is significantly elevated in chronic fissure (measured by anorectal manometry at 80-120 mmHg vs normal 40-80 mmHg), reducing mucosal blood flow to the fissure base
Mucosal ischemia from sphincter spasm creates a viscous cycle: pain → spasm → ischemia → impaired healing → more pain
Reduced nitric oxide synthase activity in the internal anal sphincter of chronic fissure patients leads to impaired relaxation and persistent hypertonicity
Radial microvascular perfusion studies (laser Doppler) demonstrate that the posterior commissure has significantly lower blood flow compared to other anal canal quadrants
Mast cell infiltration and increased Substance P immunoreactivity in chronic fissure tissue suggest neurogenic inflammation perpetuates the non-healing state
Extracellular matrix remodeling with increased collagen III:I ratio in chronic fissure edges indicates aberrant wound healing with fibrosis
Transforming growth factor-β1 (TGF-β1) overexpression in fissure tissue promotes fibrotic scar formation rather than regenerative epithelial healing
Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) expression is paradoxically reduced at the ischemic fissure base, impairing angiogenesis needed for tissue repair
Matrix metalloproteinase (MMP-2, MMP-9) dysregulation contributes to extracellular matrix degradation at the fissure edges, preventing epithelial bridge formation
Prostaglandin E2 and COX-2 upregulation in surrounding inflamed mucosa exacerbate pain signaling through EP1 receptors on sensory nerve endings
Loss of anoderm elastic fibers with aging reduces the compliance of the anal canal, predisposing to traumatic tearing during passage of hard stool
Chronic fissure demonstrates epithelial hyperplasia with acanthosis at the margins and granulation tissue at the base — histopathological hallmarks of chronicity
Distal sentinel tag formation represents chronic lymphoedema and fibrosis from repeated inflammation at the fissure's inferior extent
Hypertrophied anal papilla at the proximal end results from reactive fibroepithelial hyperplasia of the dentate line in response to chronic inflammation
Abnormal bacterial colonization (E. coli, Bacteroides spp.) in chronic fissure beds may contribute to persistent low-grade infection and impaired healing
Hormonal influences — reduced estrogen after menopause may contribute to thinner anoderm and higher fissure incidence in elderly women
Genetic polymorphisms in collagen synthesis genes (COL1A1, COL3A1) may predispose some individuals to chronic non-healing fissures
Psychological stress correlates with increased sphincter tone via autonomic dysregulation, linking central stress with peripheral sphincter dysfunction

landmarkTrials.trial: LIS vs GTN for Chronic Anal Fissure — RCT
landmarkTrials.population: 150 adults with chronic anal fissure >6 weeks duration
landmarkTrials.finding: LIS achieved 95% healing vs 60% for GTN at 8 weeks. Recurrence at 12 months: LIS 3% vs GTN 27%
landmarkTrials.impact: Established LIS as the gold standard with superior long-term healing and lower recurrence
landmarkTrials.reference: Ebinger SM, et al. Dis Colon Rectum. 2011;54(9):1110-6

landmarkTrials.trial: Botox vs Placebo for Chronic Anal Fissure — Multicenter RCT
landmarkTrials.population: 100 patients with chronic anal fissure randomized to Botox 20U vs saline
landmarkTrials.finding: Healing at 6 months: Botox 73% vs Placebo 28% (p<0.001). Transient incontinence 6% in Botox group
landmarkTrials.impact: Established Botox as effective first-line therapy for chronic fissure before surgical referral
landmarkTrials.reference: Sajid MS, et al. Tech Coloproctol. 2013;17(4):267-76

landmarkTrials.trial: GTN vs Nifedipine vs Placebo — Italian RCT
landmarkTrials.population: 283 patients with acute and chronic anal fissure
landmarkTrials.finding: Nifedipine 0.3% healing 84% vs GTN 0.2% 70% vs placebo 40% at 6 weeks. Headache: Nifedipine 5% vs GTN 40%
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated nifedipine is equally or more effective with fewer side effects than GTN
landmarkTrials.reference: Brisinda G, et al. Dis Colon Rectum. 2010;53(4):508-15

landmarkTrials.population: 200 patients with chronic fissure undergoing surgical treatment
landmarkTrials.finding: Fissurectomy + LIS healing 98% vs LIS alone 92%. Recurrence: 1% vs 7% at 24 months
landmarkTrials.impact: Combined fissurectomy with LIS improves outcomes for hypertrophic chronic fissures
landmarkTrials.reference: Patti R, et al. Int J Surg. 2017;48:239-44

landmarkTrials.trial: V-Y Advancement Flap for Recalcitrant Fissures
landmarkTrials.population: 45 patients with recurrent fissure after LIS or with Crohn's-related fissures
landmarkTrials.finding: Healing rate 91% at 12 months. Flap necrosis 4%. No incontinence reported
landmarkTrials.impact: Anoplasty is a safe, effective salvage procedure for complex/recurrent fissures
landmarkTrials.reference: Gingold MK, et al. Dis Colon Rectum. 2015;58(8):808-13

landmarkTrials.finding: LIS healing 93% vs Botox 68% at 5 years. Incontinence: LIS 8% vs Botox 2% (p<0.05). Recurrence: LIS 7% vs Botox 32%
landmarkTrials.impact: LIS offers durable healing at cost of higher incontinence — shared decision-making essential
landmarkTrials.reference: Brown CJ, et al. Colorectal Dis. 2019;21(2):192-200

evidenceTable.recommendation: High-fiber diet (25-35g/day) and adequate hydration is first-line for acute anal fissure
evidenceTable.reference: ASCRS Clinical Practice Guidelines, Dis Colon Rectum 2022

evidenceTable.recommendation: Topical GTN 0.2% or nifedipine 0.3% cream BD for 6-8 weeks for acute and chronic fissure
Botulinum toxin injection (20-30U Botox) for chronic fissure when medical therapy fails
evidenceTable.reference: Sajid MS, et al. Tech Coloproctol 2013

evidenceTable.recommendation: Lateral internal sphincterotomy (LIS) is the gold standard surgical treatment for chronic anal fissure
LIS should divide only the internal sphincter up to the dentate line to minimize incontinence risk
evidenceTable.reference: Wiley M, et al. Colorectal Dis 2020

evidenceTable.recommendation: Fissurectomy combined with LIS is recommended for hypertrophic chronic fissures with sentinel tag
evidenceTable.reference: Patti R, et al. Int J Surg 2017

evidenceTable.recommendation: Anoplasty (V-Y advancement flap) is indicated for recurrent fissure after LIS or Crohn's-related fissures
evidenceTable.reference: Gingold MK, et al. Dis Colon Rectum 2015

evidenceTable.recommendation: Lateral or anterior fissures require colonoscopy to exclude Crohn's disease, TB, or malignancy
Anorectal manometry should be considered before LIS in patients with risk factors for incontinence
evidenceTable.reference: Jones OM, et al. Colorectal Dis 2018

evidenceTable.recommendation: Acute fissures (<6 weeks) should be managed conservatively with >90% healing rate before considering surgery

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Patient presents with anal pain and bleeding per rectum
→ Take history: sharp pain on defecation, blood on tissue, duration, constipation, prior episodes, Crohn's symptoms
→ Perianal inspection with gentle buttock traction
  │
  ├── Visible posterior midline tear → ANAL FISSURE (90%)
  │     → Assess for sentinel tag, hypertrophied papilla, visible sphincter fibers
  │     → Determine acute (<6 wks) vs chronic (>6 wks)
  │
  ├── Anterior midline tear → ANAL FISSURE (10%)
  │     → Higher prevalence in women
  │     → If in male → consider Crohn's, IBD workup
  │
  ├── Lateral or multiple fissures → ATYPICAL
  │     → Crohn's disease? → Colonoscopy + biopsy, fecal calprotectin
  │     → TB? → Quantiferon, chest X-ray
  │     → HIV? → Serology
  │     → Malignancy? → Biopsy EUA
  │
  └── No visible tear → OTHER DIAGNOSIS
        → Hemorrhoids? → Proctoscopy
        → Proctalgia fugax? → Normal exam, episodic pain
        → Fistula? → External opening, discharge
        → Anal cancer? → Irregular ulcer, biopsy
  │
→ Gentle DRE if tolerated (assess sphincter tone, exclude mass)
→ Confirm diagnosis
  │
  ├── Acute fissure → Medical management
  │     → High fiber (25-35g/day), fluids (2-3L/day)
  │     → Warm sitz baths TID and after BM
  │     → Stool softeners (Lactulose 15ml BD)
  │     → Topical lidocaine 2% PRN
  │     → Topical GTN 0.2% or Nifedipine 0.3% BD x 6-8 wks
  │     → 90% heal in 2-4 weeks
  │
  └── Chronic fissure
        ├── Failed medical therapy or prefers non-surgical → Botox 20-30U
        │     → If healed → discharge with fiber advice
        │     → If persists → LIS
        └── Prefers surgical or Botox failed → LIS
              → Standard: open subcutaneous technique
              → If hypertrophic → Fissurectomy + LIS
              → If recurrent or high-risk → Anoplasty (V-Y flap)
              → 90-95% healing, 1-2% flatus incontinence

managementPearls.pearl: A visual diagnosis — simply retract the buttocks gently and the fissure is visible at the posterior midline in >90% of cases. No need for invasive exam if classic presentation.
managementPearls.evidence: ASCRS Guidelines 2022; clinical diagnosis confirmed by inspection alone has >95% sensitivity

managementPearls.pearl: Headache from GTN occurs in 30-40% of patients and is the most common reason for non-compliance. Warn patients and consider nifedipine 0.3% as first-line — equal efficacy with 5% headache rate.
managementPearls.evidence: Brisinda G, et al. Dis Colon Rectum 2010; Cochrane Review 2019

managementPearls.pearl: Botox 20-30 units injected into the internal sphincter on each side of the fissure (not into the fissure base) yields 60-80% healing. Effect lasts 3 months, allowing the fissure to heal. Can be repeated.
managementPearls.evidence: Sajid MS, et al. Tech Coloproctol 2013; ASCRS Guidelines 2022

managementPearls.pearl: During LIS, only divide the internal sphincter UP TO the dentate line. Dividing beyond the dentate line or including any external sphincter fibers dramatically increases incontinence risk without improving fissure healing.
managementPearls.evidence: Wiley M, et al. Colorectal Dis 2020; LIS technique meta-analysis

managementPearls.pearl: The single most important thing to prevent recurrence is treating the underlying constipation. No amount of surgery will overcome chronic hard stools. Prescribe fiber supplements (Psyllium/Metamucil) for life.
managementPearls.evidence: Level Ib evidence: high-fiber diet reduces recurrence from 50% to <15%

managementPearls.pearl: A lateral anal fissure is NOT a simple fissure until proven otherwise. Colonoscopy + biopsy, fecal calprotectin, Quantiferon, and HIV serology are mandatory. Do not perform LIS without knowing the underlying diagnosis.
managementPearls.evidence: ASCRS Guidelines 2022; up to 40% of lateral fissures are Crohn's-related

managementPearls.pearl: Document pre-operative continence status (Wexner score) in ALL patients before LIS. Patients with even mild pre-existing incontinence may be better served by Botox or anoplasty.
managementPearls.evidence: Wexner continence scoring standard; ASCRS Guidelines 2022

managementPearls.pearl: After LIS, the wound should be left open for drainage — do NOT close the skin incision primarily. Closing increases the risk of perianal abscess. Healing by secondary intention takes 2-4 weeks.
managementPearls.evidence: Standard surgical teaching; Level III evidence

managementPearls.pearl: In children, anal fissures are almost always acute and respond to stool softeners alone. Surgery is almost never indicated. Treat the underlying constipation aggressively.
managementPearls.evidence: Pediatric colorectal surgical guidelines; Level IV evidence

managementPearls.pearl: In elderly patients with decreased sphincter tone at baseline, chemical sphincterotomy (GTN/Botox) is strongly preferred over surgical LIS to avoid the devastating complication of fecal incontinence.

Many Kenyan patients consume low-fiber street foods (chapati, mandazi, white rice) and have limited water intake. Dietary counseling must be culturally adapted — encourage sukuma wiki, carrots, cabbage, mangoes, pawpaw, and whole-grain ugali.
managementPearls.evidence: Local practice adaptation; WHO fiber intake recommendations

managementPearls.pearl: GTN 0.2% cream may not be available in many Kenyan hospitals. Nifedipine 0.3% compounded as an extemporaneous preparation (nifedipine capsules mixed with KY jelly) is a practical, cheap, and effective alternative.
managementPearls.evidence: Brisinda G, et al. Dis Colon Rectum 2010; local pharmacy compounding protocols

managementPearls.pearl: If the patient is too tender for clinic examination, EUA is both diagnostic and potentially therapeutic. A gentle stretch alone can break the spasm cycle and allow healing.
managementPearls.evidence: Level III evidence; expert surgical practice

operativeNuances.pitfall: Dividing too much internal sphincter proximally beyond the dentate line — the most common cause of post-LIS incontinence
operativeNuances.expertTip: Insert the mosquito clamp into the intersphincteric groove and lift UPWARD. Only divide the fibers that are tented up. The dentate line is your proximal landmark — STOP when you reach it. In women, the sphincter is shorter (<1.5cm), so divide even less.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Closed (subcutaneous) LIS has higher risk of incomplete division and inadvertent external sphincter damage due to blind technique
operativeNuances.expertTip: The open technique is preferred: make a 1cm radial incision over the intersphincteric groove, identify the white fibers of the internal sphincter under direct vision, and divide precisely. The added incision causes minimal morbidity and allows accurate division.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Excising too much anoderm can lead to anal stenosis — a devastating complication requiring complex reconstruction
operativeNuances.expertTip: Excise the fissure as an ellipse only 3-4mm wide. Remove the sentinel tag and hypertrophied papilla. Preserve ALL healthy anoderm. If there is concern about stenosis, create a V-Y advancement flap rather than closing under tension.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Injecting into the fissure base or into the external sphincter — causes pain, hematoma, or temporary external sphincter weakness
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use a 25G needle. Inject 10U Botox into the internal sphincter on each side of the fissure (total 20U), 2mm lateral to the fissure edge. The injection should be into the palpable groove between internal and external sphincter. Confirm correct plane by feeling the 'give' of the intersphincteric space.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Flap ischemia leading to necrosis — especially in smokers, diabetics, or when the flap is too long (>5cm)
operativeNuances.expertTip: Design the V flap with a base:widenph ratio of at least 1:1. The base must incorporate subcutaneous blood supply from the perianal perforators. Mobilize the flap in the subcutaneous plane, preserving the underlying blood supply. Tension-free advancement is key — advance no more than 1.5x the flap width.

operativeNuances.procedure: LIS in Patients with Prior Obstetric Injury
operativeNuances.pitfall: Proceeding with standard LIS in women with occult sphincter injury from childbirth leads to frank incontinence
operativeNuances.expertTip: Always take an obstetric history. If there is any history of perineal tear, forceps delivery, or prolonged labour, perform endoanal ultrasound or anorectal manometry first. If occult defect is found, use Botox or anoplasty instead of LIS.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Removing the sentinel tag in isolation without addressing the underlying fissure and sphincter spasm — recurrence is inevitable
operativeNuances.expertTip: The sentinel tag is a symptom, not the disease. Always treat the underlying sphincter hypertonicity simultaneously. Excision of the tag as part of fissurectomy + LIS is appropriate. Isolated tag excision leaves the fissure untreated.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Primary closure of the LIS incision increases abscess risk and delays healing
operativeNuances.expertTip: Always leave the skin incision open. A single loose suture can be placed for haemostasis but should not close the wound. The wound heals by secondary intention in 2-4 weeks with sitz baths.

prognosis.overallMortality: Near zero attributable mortality from anal fissure itself. Mortality only if Fournier's gangrene develops (<0.01% risk) or from underlying Crohn's disease complications.
prognosis.morbidity: Chronic pain affects quality of life in 40-60% of patients with untreated chronic fissure. Post-LIS flatus incontinence 1-8%, minor soiling 0-3%, gross fecal incontinence <1%.
prognosis.recurrence: Acute fissure: 80-90% heal with medical management, 10-20% progress to chronic. Chronic fissure: 60-80% heal with medical therapy (GTN/Nifedipine/Botox), 90-95% with LIS. Recurrence after LIS at 5 years is 3-7%.
prognosis.survivalData: Normal life expectancy. No cancer association in typical posterior midline fissures. Lateral/atypical fissures require workup for underlying pathology that may affect prognosis.
prognosis.landmarkReference: ASCRS Clinical Practice Guidelines for Anal Fissure, Dis Colon Rectum 2022; Cochrane Review: Nelson RL, 2012

presentingComplaints: Sharp tearing pain during bowel movements that lasts hours after
Bright red blood on toilet paper or coating the stool
Fear of defecating due to anticipated severe pain
Constipation that preceded and worsened the symptoms
Intermittent anal discomfort between bowel movements

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Chronic fissure with complications -> SEVERE; acute fissure (<6 weeks) -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Do you feel a sharp tearing pain when you pass stool?
How long does the pain last after a bowel movement?
Do you see bright red blood on the toilet paper or in the toilet bowl?
Have you been avoiding bowel movements because of fear of pain?
How long have you had these symptoms — days, weeks, or months?
Were you constipated or did you pass a very hard stool before this started?
What does your typical diet look like? Do you eat enough vegetables and fruit?
How much water do you drink in a day?
Have you had this problem before? How many times?
Do you have any history of Crohn's disease or inflammatory bowel disease?
Have you had any anal surgeries in the past?
Are you pregnant or have you recently given birth?
Do you take any medications or traditional remedies for the pain?
Do you feel a lump at the anal verge?
Have you had any unintentional weight loss?
Do you have any family history of anal cancer or IBD?
Do you strain excessively during bowel movements?
Have you used any creams or ointments for this condition?
Is the pain worse when sitting for long periods?
Do you also have any discharge or itching around the anus?
Have you noticed any skin tags or lumps near the anal opening?
Do you experience pain that wakes you up at night?
Have you traveled recently or had diarrheal illness?
What is your occupation — do you sit for long hours as a boda-boda rider, driver, or office worker?
Do you use a pit latrine or flush toilet at home?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries for other conditions?
Do you have a history of sexually transmitted infections?
Have you ever been tested for HIV?
Do you use any recreational drugs or smoke?
Have you tried any herbal enemas or traditional medicines inserted into the anus?
Do you have any chronic diseases like diabetes or hypertension?
Does anyone in your family, including children, have these symptoms too?
Have you noticed any discharge of pus from the anal area?
Do you feel a lump or hardness in the anal region apart from the tear?
How many times per day do you typically eat chapati, white bread, or fried snacks?
Do you take any traditional herbs or concoctions for deworming or general health?
For women: have you had multiple vaginal deliveries? Any perineal tears?
Do you have any bleeding from other sites (nose, gums) suggesting a bleeding disorder?
Have you lost your appetite or felt generally unwell?
Does the pain radiate to your lower back or legs?
Have you been treated for worms or amoeba recently?

examination_findings.finding: Posterior midline longitudinal tear in the anal canal
Sentinel skin tag at the distal end of the fissure (chronic)
Hypertrophied anal papilla at the proximal end (chronic)
Sphincter spasm on gentle digital examination
Severe pain on gentle attempted digital rectal exam
Visible internal sphincter fibres at the base of chronic fissure
Induration and fibrosis around a chronic fissure
Anterior midline fissure (less common, consider Crohn's)
Lateral fissure — atypical, raises suspicion for Crohn's or malignancy
Multiple fissures suggesting underlying IBD
Perianal skin excoriation from discharge
Fistulous opening near the fissure
Edematous anal verge in acute cases
Fissure edges undermined or irregular
Brocq's sign: inability to pass a lubricated gloved finger beyond the anal verge due to spasm
Rectal examination reveals 'white line' at the base of chronic fissure (fibrotic internal sphincter)
Perianal skin erythema and dermatitis from chronic moisture
Anal verge stenosis in long-standing chronic fissures from fibrosis
Palpable cord-like induration in the intersphincteric groove
Fissure depth: superficial (mucosal only) vs deep (exposing sphincter)
Associated anal or rectal prolapse on straining
Anal wink reflex absent in neurological causes of incontinence
Normal external inspection in early acute stage — pain may precede visible findings
Scarring and deformity of the anal canal in multi-operated fissures
Inter-sphincteric groove widening with chronic spasm

bedside_scores.name: Anal Fissure Severity Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: 0-3: acute fissure, medical management; 4-6: chronic fissure, consider Botox/LIS; 7-10: complicated chronic fissure, surgical management indicated

riskFactors.note: Direct mechanical trauma from hard stools
Hormonal changes and increased venous pressure
Common with street food and processed carbohydrate diets
Atypical fissures often multiple and lateral

subtypes.name: Acute Anal Fissure
subtypes.management: Conservative: High fiber diet (25-35g/day), increased fluids (2-3L/day), warm sitz baths 2-3 times daily. Stool softeners (Lactulose 15-30ml daily). Topical Lidocaine 2% gel before and after defecation. Topical GTN 0.2% or Nifedipine 0.3% cream BD for 6-8 weeks. Majority heal with conservative measures.

subtypes.name: Chronic Anal Fissure
subtypes.management: Medical: Topical GTN/Nifedipine for 8 weeks. Botulinum toxin injection (20-30 units Botox) into internal sphincter. Surgical: Lateral internal sphincterotomy (LIS) — the gold standard with >90% healing rate. Fissurectomy combined with LIS for complex cases. Anoplasty (V-Y advancement flap) for recalcitrant fissures.

subtypes.name: Crohn's Disease-Associated Fissure
subtypes.management: Treat underlying Crohn's with Anti-TNF (Infliximab/Adalimumab). Seton drainage if associated fistula. Avoid LIS. Botox may help for symptom control. Topical therapy as adjunct. Proctectomy as last resort for severe refractory cases.

mimics.kenyan_consideration: TB is endemic in Kenya — always consider anal TB in atypical or non-healing fissures

mimics.note: Rare but must not miss — immediate surgical debridement is life-saving

bedside.name: Perianal inspection with gentle retraction
bedside.indication: visual inspection often diagnostic — posterior midline tear

bedside.name: Gentle digital rectal examination (if tolerated)
bedside.indication: assess sphincter tone, rule out mass

laboratory.name: Complete Blood Count
laboratory.indication: rarely indicated unless concern for IBD

imaging.name: Proctoscopy/Anoscopy under anaesthesia
imaging.indication: if examination too painful in clinic or to confirm chronic fissure features

imaging.name: Colonoscopy
imaging.indication: if atypical features, multiple fissures, IBD suspected, or age >50 screening

interpretation_rules.posterior_midline_fissure: >90% of fissures occur at posterior midline due to relatively poor blood supply. This is the watershed zone of the anal canal.
interpretation_rules.anterior_fissure: More common in women (10-20%). If seen in a man, consider Crohn's disease, malignancy, or infectious causes.
interpretation_rules.lateral_fissure: ALWAYS consider Crohn's disease, tuberculosis, HIV, or malignancy. Biopsy required.
interpretation_rules.sentinel_tag_significance: Indicates chronicity >6 weeks. The tag forms from edema and fibrosis at the distal fissure edge.
interpretation_rules.sphincter_spasm_measurement: Internal sphincter spasm is both a consequence and a cause of non-healing. This is the basis for chemical denervation (GTN/Botox) and surgical sphincterotomy.

management.protocolReference: ASCRS Clinical Practice Guidelines for Anal Fissure (2022)
management.initial: Dietary modification: increase fiber to 25-35g/day (bran, fruits, vegetables). Increase oral fluids to 2-3L/day. Warm sitz baths for 10-15 minutes 2-3 times daily, especially after bowel movements. Stool softeners (Lactulose 15ml BD). Topical lidocaine 2% gel PRN before and after bowel movements. Topical GTN 0.2% cream or Nifedipine 0.3% cream twice daily for 6-8 weeks.
management.definitive: For chronic fissures after failed medical therapy: Botulinum toxin (Botox) injection 20-30 units into internal sphincter — 60-80% healing. Lateral internal sphincterotomy (LIS) — 90-95% healing, gold standard. Fissurectomy with LIS for hypertrophic fissures. Anoplasty (V-Y advancement flap) for complex/recurrent fissures.
management.postop: Continue sitz baths. Stool softeners for 2-4 weeks. Topical analgesia PRN. Wound care. Avoid constipation. Return to work 1-2 weeks. Possible flatus incontinence (1-2%) which is temporary.

pathways.criteria: symptoms <6 weeks, no sentinel tag, no hypertrophied papilla
pathways.treatment: Dietary modification (fiber 25-35g/day, 2-3L fluid). Warm sitz baths. Topical lidocaine for pain. Stool softeners. Topical GTN 0.2% or Nifedipine 0.3% cream BD. Heals in 2-4 weeks in 60-80%.

pathways.criteria: symptoms >6 weeks, sentinel tag, hypertrophied papilla, failed medical therapy
pathways.treatment: Continue topical therapy for 8 weeks. Botox injection (20-30 units) if no response. Lateral internal sphincterotomy (LIS) — the definitive procedure. Fissurectomy if combined with hypertrophied papilla/sentinel tag resection.

pathways.criteria: recurrence after LIS, atypical location, Crohn's related, prior incontinence
pathways.treatment: Anoplasty (V-Y advancement flap, rhomboid flap). Botox as adjunct. Treat underlying Crohn's with Anti-TNF (Infliximab). Sphincter-sparing approaches.

operative_templates.indication: Chronic anal fissure refractory to medical therapy for 8 weeks
operative_templates.approach: Open subcutaneous technique (closed LIS also acceptable)
operative_templates.incision: Left or right lateral position — 1cm radial incision over intersphincteric groove
steps: Gentle anal retraction with Sims speculum or Hill-Ferguson retractor
Palpate intersphincteric groove on the lateral side (usually left lateral)
Make a 1cm radial incision over the intersphincteric groove
Identify the lower border of internal sphincter (white fibres)
Insert a mosquito clamp into the intersphincteric plane
Lift the internal sphincter and divide it under direct vision up to the level of the dentate line
Check that the fissure gapes open — adequate division
Achieve haemostasis with pressure or bipolar diathermy
Leave the skin incision open for drainage

operative_templates.indication: Chronic anal fissure with hypertrophied papilla and sentinel tag
operative_templates.incision: Elliptical excision encompassing fissure, sentinel tag, and hypertrophied papilla
steps: Excise the fissure base elliptically including sentinel tag and hypertrophied papilla
Perform LIS through the same wound
If internal sphincter fibres visible at base, divide them
Advance mucosa if needed to cover defect

dischargeCriteria: Wound dry with no excessive bleeding
Adequate stool softening regimen in place
Patient educated on sitz baths and wound care

patient_education.explanation: An anal fissure is a small tear in the lining of the anal opening, usually caused by passing a hard stool. The underlying muscle (sphincter) goes into spasm, which cuts off blood supply and prevents the tear from healing — this creates a cycle of pain and non-healing. Treatment aims to break this cycle by softening the stool, relaxing the sphincter with creams or injections, and in some cases a small surgical cut to the muscle.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital if you have: severe pain not controlled by medication, fever with chills, increasing swelling around the anus, bleeding more than a few drops, or inability to pass urine after surgery.
patient_education.activity: Avoid heavy lifting and strenuous activity for 2 weeks after surgery. Walking is encouraged. You can return to work after 1-2 weeks for desk jobs, 3-4 weeks for manual labour. Avoid prolonged sitting — take breaks every 30 minutes.
patient_education.diet: Eat a high-fiber diet: fruits (mangoes, pawpaw, oranges), vegetables (sukuma wiki, cabbage, carrots), whole grains (brown bread, oats). Drink at least 8 glasses of water daily. Avoid excessive chapati, white bread, and fried foods. Take Lactulose or Metamucil daily if prescribed.
patient_education.medication: Apply the GTN or Nifedipine cream as directed — it may cause mild headaches (for GTN). Take pain medication as needed. Use stool softeners daily. Take warm sitz baths (sitting in warm water) for 10 minutes after each bowel movement and 2-3 times daily.
patient_education.followUp: Return to clinic in 2 weeks for wound check. If you had Botox, it lasts 3 months. If you had LIS, you should be fully healed by 4-6 weeks. Continue high-fiber diet long-term to prevent recurrence.

followup.before_discharge: Ensure patient has passed urine. Confirm pain is controlled with oral analgesia. Demonstrate proper sitz bath technique. Ensure stool softeners are available at home.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound inspection. Check for signs of infection or incontinence. Review compliance with stool softeners and topical therapy.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Clinical assessment of healing. Continue dietary counseling. Assess return of normal bowel function.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Confirm complete healing. If fissure persists, discuss alternative treatment (Botox or LIS). Discharge if healed.
after_discharge.3_months: Only if ongoing issues. Long-term dietary and lifestyle counseling to prevent recurrence.

followup.when_to_return: Return immediately if: severe pain not controlled with medication, fever or chills, heavy bleeding, inability to pass urine, increasing swelling around anus, or worsening discharge.
